one_line_takeaway: A multi-turn jailbreak that decomposes, obfuscates, and later re-summarizes a malicious query beats single-shot jailbreaks on GPT-4 (4.38/3.14 harmfulness/executability) and, unlike them, resists degradation over a year of model updates - though it, like all tested attacks, fails against the deliberately over-refusing Llama2-13b.
key_finding: Imposter.AI achieves the top harmfulness (4.38) and executability (3.14) on GPT-4 among all methods, and stays competitive on GPT-3.5-turbo (4.36 harmfulness, 3.00 executability), where AIM slightly edges it on harmfulness (4.74). In the technique ablation on GPT-4, Perspective Change is by far the strongest obfuscation technique (4.21 harmfulness vs 1.17 for Intent Reversion), so it is chosen as the primary; combining Perspective Change + decomposition + Fictional Scenario lifts GPT-4 harmfulness to 4.57. Crucially, unlike AIM whose acceptance rate decays over time as defenses patch known patterns, Imposter.AI's acceptance rate stays roughly constant Mar 2023-Jan 2024, suggesting conversational intent-hiding is harder to detect. All methods (including Imposter.AI, 1.04 harmfulness) fail against Llama2-13b - but the paper shows this is because Llama2 rejects even safe rephrased questions (only 10% acceptance of harmless prompts vs 98% for GPT-4), i.e. it trades usability for safety rather than being robustly aligned.
core_contribution: Imposter.AI is an automated, multi-turn adversarial attack pipeline against aligned LLMs that, unlike prior jailbreaks that chase an explicitly malicious single response, hides malicious intent across a benign-looking conversation and only reconstitutes harmful content via a final summarization prompt. The paper's own novelty is three composable strategies: (1) Harmful Question Decomposition - breaking one malicious question into 5-7 less-toxic sub-questions (generated from an uncensored LLM's keypoints via a two-stage 'broaden term then form question' rewrite, then sorted benign-to-harmful); (2) Question Toxicity Reduction - obfuscating each sub-question via Perspective Change, Intent Reversion, or Concept Substitution; and (3) Response Harmfulness Enhancement - amplifying answers via Fictional Scenarios, Historical Examples, or Concept Reintroduction. An 'agent LLM' (GPT-4) mechanically executes the rewrites. The paper also reframes evaluation: instead of binary Attack Success Rate, it scores the *information* in responses on Harmfulness and Executability (1-5). Note: red-teaming/uncensored-model framing and the baselines (GCG, AIM, combination3) are cited Related Work, not this paper's contribution.
threat_model: A malicious user with black-box query access to an aligned, safety-tuned LLM (GPT-3.5-turbo, GPT-4) wants operational harmful information (e.g., weapons, drugs, crime) that the model would refuse if asked directly. The attacker additionally has access to a locally-run uncensored open model (WizardLM-1.0-Uncensored-Llama2-13b) to seed rough keypoints, and an agent LLM (GPT-4) to perform rewrites. The attack requires no gradients or model internals; it exploits the model's limited ability to correlate separate sub-questions across a conversation and its tendency to answer literal phrasing, evading input/output content filters because no single turn is overtly malicious. Harm is realized only when the attacker asks the target to summarize the whole dialogue.
limitations: Evaluation is small-scale and human-judgment-based: only 180 questions for the main comparison (50 for ablations) from a single dataset (HarmfulQ), scored by three internal collaborators, and executability agreement is only moderate (Fleiss Kappa ~0.55). The pipeline depends on access to an uncensored seed model and uses GPT-4 both as the agent LLM and as a target, a potential confound. Results are English-only and limited to three (partly dated) model versions; the temporal 'acceptance rate' uses a crude keyword heuristic. The paper offers no defense, only posing the open question of how to detect malicious ultimate intent across a multi-turn dialogue. As a dual-use attack paper it directly documents techniques to elicit weapons/drug/crime instructions.

datasets_used: HarmfulQ (Shaikh et al., 2023) - 200 explicit harmful questions in English; split into Opt set (20, prompt optimization), Main set (180, baseline comparison), Sub set (50, technique/combination ablations)
Questions categorized using the harmful-question taxonomy of Ganguli et al. (2022)
quantitative_results: Baseline comparison on GPT-4 (Imposter.AI): Harmfulness 4.38, Executability 3.14 - top among all methods
Baseline comparison on GPT-3.5-turbo (Imposter.AI): Harmfulness 4.36, Executability 3.00; AIM edges harmfulness at 4.74
GCG on GPT-4: Harmfulness 1.34, Executability 1.30; combination3 on GPT-4: Harmfulness 1.80, Executability 1.47
On Llama2-13b all methods fail (Imposter.AI 1.04 harmfulness / 1.10 executability)
Technique analysis on GPT-4: Perspective Change 4.21 harm / 3.24 exec; Intent Reversion 1.17 / 1.31; Fictional Scenario 1.6 / 1.47; Historical Example 1.4 / 1.31
Combination on GPT-4: Perspective Change alone 4.21/3.24; +decomposition 4.30/3.29; +Fictional Scenario 4.57/3.41; +Historical Example 4.41/3.39
Llama2-13b safety/usability probe (Table 2): acceptance of original harmful prompts 0%, of harmless rephrased prompts only 10% (vs GPT-4: 0% orig, 98% safe)
Inter-annotator agreement (Fleiss' Kappa, overall): Harmfulness 0.743 by score / 0.876 by class; Executability 0.554 by score / 0.560 by class
Temporal study: AIM acceptance rate declines Mar 2023-Jan 2024 while Imposter.AI stays roughly constant
baselines_compared: AIM - manually crafted jailbreak prompt (role-play with no ethical constraints), from jailbreakchat.com
combination3 (Wei et al., 2023) - combined prefix injection + refusal suppression in one prompt
GCG (Zou et al., 2023) - greedy/gradient-based search for an adversarial jailbreak suffix
